era_context: The late 15th century marks the early phase of European global expansion, following Columbus's 1492 voyage. The Ottoman Empire dominates the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans under Bayezid II. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty (1368–1644) is in its consolidation phase under the Zhengde Emperor (r. 1505–1521), though regency politics dominate. In Europe, the Italian Wars have begun (1494–1559), reshaping the political landscape of the peninsula and drawing in major powers. This is the cusp of the Renaissance in Italy and the early modern period globally.

events.title: John Cabot's Voyage to North America
events.description: John Cabot (Giovanni Caboto), a Venetian explorer commissioned by Henry VII of England, sailed from Bristol in May 1496 and reached the North American coast, likely present-day Newfoundland or Cape Breton Island. This voyage, documented in contemporary letters and later chronicles, established an English claim to North America and demonstrated the feasibility of a northern transatlantic route. Cabot's expedition was smaller and less well-documented than Columbus's 1492 voyage but proved significant for English colonial ambitions in the New World.

sources.name: Letter from John Day to Columbus (c. 1497–1498), describing Cabot's voyage
Historiographic consensus from modern scholarship (Alwyn Ruddock, David Quinn)

events.certainty_note: The voyage itself is confirmed by royal letters patent and contemporary correspondence. The exact landfall location (Newfoundland vs. Cape Breton vs. further south) remains debated; 1496 is the accepted date of departure, though some sources place the voyage in 1497.

events.title: Portuguese Expedition to India: Vasco da Gama Departs Lisbon
events.description: Vasco da Gama departed Lisbon in July 1496 with a fleet of four ships, commissioned by King Manuel I to find a sea route to India around the Cape of Good Hope. This expedition, which would reach India in May 1498, marked the beginning of direct European maritime trade with Asia and fundamentally altered global commerce and geopolitics. The 1496 departure is the starting point of this transformative voyage.
key_figures: Bartolomeu Dias (advisor, previous explorer of the Cape)

sources.name: Roteiro (journal) of Vasco da Gama's voyage, compiled by an anonymous crew member
Royal records of Portugal (Arquivo Nacional da Torre do Tombo)
Historiographic consensus from Portuguese and international scholarship

events.certainty_note: The departure date of July 8, 1496, is well-documented in contemporary Portuguese royal records and the Roteiro. This is one of the most securely dated events of the age of exploration.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias's rounding of the Cape of Good Hope)

events.title: Expulsion of Jews from Spain (Continuation and Enforcement)
events.description: The expulsion of Jews from Spain, decreed in March 1492 by the Catholic Monarchs Ferdinand and Isabella, was enforced throughout 1492–1496. By 1496, the expulsion was largely complete, with tens of thousands of Spanish Jews (Sephardim) having fled to Portugal, North Africa, the Ottoman Empire, and other destinations. This event, part of the broader Spanish Inquisition and Reconquista consolidation, had profound demographic, economic, and cultural consequences for both Spain and the diaspora communities.

sources.name: Edicto de Granada (Edict of Expulsion, March 31, 1492)
Contemporary Jewish chronicles and responsa (rabbinic legal opinions)
Ottoman and North African records documenting refugee arrivals

events.certainty_note: The expulsion decree is contemporary and well-documented. The enforcement timeline (1492–1496) is established by multiple sources. However, the total number of expellees (estimates range from 50,000 to 200,000) remains debated.

events.title: Battle of the Taro (Italian Wars)
events.region: Northern Italy (Taro River valley, near Parma)
events.description: On July 6, 1496, French forces under King Charles VIII defeated a coalition of Italian states (Milan, Venice, the Papacy, and others) at the Battle of the Taro. This engagement, though tactically inconclusive, demonstrated French military superiority and reinforced the precarious position of Italian powers. The battle was part of the broader Italian Wars (1494–1559), which reshaped European politics and military strategy.
key_figures: Ludovico Sforza, Duke of Milan (coalition leader)

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles by Italian historians (e.g., Marino Sanuto)
French royal records and military accounts
Historiographic consensus from Italian and European military historians

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-documented in contemporary chronicles and military records. The date (July 6, 1496) is secure.
cross_references: 1494-001 (Charles VIII's invasion of Italy, beginning the Italian Wars)
1495-001 (Battle of Fornovo, earlier engagement in the Italian Wars)

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian War (Continuation): Siege of Lepanto
events.description: The Ottoman-Venetian War (1499–1503) had not yet begun in 1496, but Ottoman naval expansion and Venetian defensive measures were intensifying. In 1496, the Ottomans under Bayezid II were consolidating control of the Aegean and preparing for conflict with Venice. This period saw increased Ottoman corsairing and Venetian fortification of key positions. The broader context of Ottoman-Venetian rivalry would culminate in the 1499–1503 war.

sources.name: Venetian state records (Archivio di Stato di Venezia)

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Ottoman-Venetian tension in 1496 is well-established. Specific military engagements in 1496 are less clearly documented than those of 1499–1503.

events.title: Zhengde Emperor Ascends Ming Throne (Regency Period Begins)
events.description: In 1496, the Zhengde Emperor (r. 1505–1521) was born; however, the regency period of the Ming Dynasty was dominated by the Hongwu Emperor's successors and court factions. This year falls within the reign of the Jiajing Emperor's predecessors, a period of internal court politics and factional struggle. The Ming Dynasty was stable but increasingly influenced by eunuch power and bureaucratic factionalism.
key_figures: Jiajing Emperor (reigning 1522–1566, but not yet emperor in 1496)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Ming Dynasty politics in 1496 is well-established. Specific events of this year are less prominent in surviving records.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of John Cabot's 1496 voyage has been reassessed by modern scholarship. While long celebrated as establishing English claims to North America, recent historians (including David Quinn and Alwyn Ruddock) note that Cabot's voyage had minimal immediate impact on English colonial efforts and that the 'discovery' narrative is a retrospective construction. The voyage was not widely publicized in England at the time and did not lead to sustained English exploration or settlement for another century. Additionally, the exact landfall location remains disputed, with some scholars arguing for a more southerly location (Nova Scotia or even further south) than the traditional Newfoundland identification. The expulsion of Jews from Spain (1492–1496) is sometimes presented as a unified, complete event, but modern scholarship emphasizes the protracted nature of enforcement, the existence of crypto-Jews who remained, and the complex negotiations and delays that characterized the process. The Battle of the Taro (1496) is often overshadowed in narratives of the Italian Wars by the more famous Battle of Fornovo (1495), and its strategic significance is debated—some historians view it as a minor engagement rather than a major turning point.
historiographic_note: The year 1496 is well-documented for European events, particularly exploration and warfare, due to the survival of royal records, chronicles, and diplomatic correspondence. However, documentation is heavily weighted toward Western Europe (Spain, Portugal, France, Italy, England). Ottoman and Eastern Mediterranean sources exist but are less abundant in Western archives. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are comprehensive but often focus on court politics and administrative matters rather than external events. African, American, and Asian sources outside the Ming sphere are sparse or absent from surviving records. The 'Age of Exploration' narrative, centered on European voyages, has historically overshadowed simultaneous developments in other regions (e.g., Chinese maritime expeditions under Zheng He, which had ended by 1433). Modern scholarship is increasingly correcting this Eurocentric bias, but the surviving documentary record remains skewed toward European perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Cabot's voyage follows Columbus's 1492 voyage and is part of the broader European exploration of the Atlantic
Da Gama's 1496 departure led to his arrival in India in 1498, establishing the sea route to Asia
Continuation of the expulsion of Jews from Spain, decreed in 1492
Battle of the Taro is part of the Italian Wars, which began with Charles VIII's invasion in 1494
Ottoman-Venetian tensions in 1496 escalated into the Ottoman-Venetian War (1499–1503)
